This keeps the analysis workers decoupled from the API layer.

review::.::holistic::cross_module_archit.summary: dump.py maintains dual state (in-memory dict + DB table) for task tracking; memprocfs.py stores live session state at module scope without DB persistence

evidence: dump.py:18: 'dump_tasks = {}' module-level dict tracks in-flight task status; dump.py also writes the same status to a 'dump_tasks' DB table (lines 134, 137). On process restart, in-memory dict is empty but DB has completed/failed records — the two sources diverge.

memprocfs.py:29: 'active_sessions = {}' module-level dict is the sole source of truth for live container sessions; there is no DB-backed recovery — sessions are lost on restart with no cleanup of orphaned Docker containers.

Neither dict is reachable from outside the route module (no service layer), so any other module that needs to query or modify session/task state must import these route modules, pulling in Flask/Docker dependencies.
detail.suggestion: For dump_tasks: remove the in-memory dict and query the DB table exclusively for status reads (the DB already has the right schema). For active_sessions in memprocfs.py: either persist session state in the DB (a 'memprocfs_sessions' table) and reconcile on startup by checking Docker for live containers, or move the dict to a dedicated service object passed through Flask's app.extensions context so it does not live at route-module scope.
